review.notes: Kept by editorial decision: technically a collocation, but idiomatic in the sense that matters (HAVE for body states, not BE). Weak as a headline entry — its earning power is the trap, not the expression: estoy caliente / sono caldo both read as arousal. Slot as a summer-season beginner day, never a showcase. Note: schema CEFR floor is A2; there is no A1 value.

bridge.note: Both languages use HAVE, not BE — and both punish the learner who reaches for BE in exactly the same way. Estoy caliente and sono caldo are each heard as a statement of arousal, not temperature. The parallel is your friend here; do not break it.

interference.es_to_it: An English-influenced Spanish speaker who has learned to avoid 'estoy caliente' sometimes still produces 'sono caldo' in Italian. Same trap, same fix: ho caldo.
interference.it_to_es: Mirrored exactly: 'estoy caliente' is the same error as 'sono caldo'. Use tengo calor.
